bibl.author: Burnand, Francis C.
bibl.title: Madame Berliot's Ball; or, Chalet in the Valley
A Comic Drama in two acts, 5 Scenes, and 6 Tableaux
Lacy's Acting Edition, volume 61, No. 0906

event.desc: Licence sent 3 December 1863 for performance at the Royalty.
Premiered at New Royalty Theatre 26 Dec. 1863

revisionDesc.change: Add wikidata link to titleStmt where possible

docTitle.titlePart: Or, The Chalet In The Valley.
A Comic Drama, In Two Acts, Five Scenes, And Six Tableaux.

performance.head: Madame Berliot'S Ball.
performance.p: First performed at the New Royalty Theatre (under the direction of Mrs. Charles Selby), on Saturday, December 26, 1863.

castItem.roleDesc: (known in certain circles as the "Old Beau")
(formerly John Finch, of the Shakespeare's Bowl, Covent Garden; now head waiter at Madame Berliot's Hotel et Pension)
Swiss Villagers, Guides, Porters, Musicians, Gentlemen and Ladies, Tourists, Waiters, &c., &c.

div.head: Scene First.—
div.stage: The Scene represents the Exterior of Madame Berliot's Hotel et Pension, Interlachen ; two sides of a Swiss house, R., are seen, and a similar two sides, L. ; between these is the garden, prettily laid out in flower beds and gravel walks ; a covered verandah runs all round the ground floor windows of both houses ; at the back there is a low ornamental fence, dividing the gardens from the road, on which two gates open and lead through the garden, R. C. and L. C., to the front of the houses by separate paths ; the fronts of the houses are unseen by the audience and are supposed to be facing the view which is seen from the fence, namely, the Walnut-tree Walk, which runs along at the back of the stage, where, through the intervals of trees, are seen views of the meadows at the foot of the distant mountains, and the entrance of the Lauterbrunnen road, while looming in the distance may be seen the virgin snows of the lofty Jungfrau ; in different parts of the garden are small round tables with marble slabs, and cane chairs ; all the windows practicable, those on the ground floor opening on to the verandah, from which there is one step into the garden.

Music.– At the rising of the curtain the scene presents a very busy appearance, but in a tableau, the characters moving from their several attitudes when the curtain has fully risen—at one table two Gentlemen are paying a Waiter ; a Lady and Gentleman have just risen from another and are about to go off at back ; the promenade under the walnut trees is quite full; one or two Beggars are selling carved wood figures; Porters are carrying luggage into the hotel, R. and L.; Peasants crossing at back to the front of the house, with baskets of fruit ; the different characters represent English, German, French, Russian, and American tourists—Edward Lester is seated by fence at back, sketching the Walnut-tree Walk and Jungfrau; Watchitt is sitting drinking at a table, R.; Marks comes down from back and examines every one carefully as he passes them; Pierre is seen through the window of the salle-à-manger under the verandah, arranging dinner table—Marks comes down.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: I'm afraid I'm on the wrong scent. (taking chair opposite side of the table at which Watchitt is seated) If you'll allow me, sir—why, Dick Watchitt, it never can be you, surely?

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: It is though, Bill Marks; and what are you doing here?—bank business?

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Not exactly. I'll just tell you, and perhaps we may be able to work into each other's hands a bit. You recollect a pair as used to go about together, husband and wife—Doddle Snaggs, the "Old Beau," as used to pretend to be so fond o' fishing?

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: And Sally Snaggs, "the Countess;" I know 'em.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: That's them. Well, their little game was to travel about, and the old fellow 'ud pretend to be a little silly, while the Countess would make some young fellow fall desperately in love with her. Her story always to them is that she was married against her will for family reasons, and that her husband is fonder of fishing than of her.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Then the young fellow takes pity on her. Ha, ha!

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Exactly ; and then they'd be just going to elope, when down comes the old fellow as everyone thought so silly and blind, and there'd be a pretty lot o' money to pay afore the pigeon 'ud get off.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Are they here?

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Yes; somewhere about. They generally manage to keep clear, but now they've been passing flimsies, and I'm after 'em for that. The worst on it is they've got some alias now as I ain't been able to hit upon ; and as for their disguises there's no end to them. It's the Countess as puts the "Old Beau" up to the work; ah! it's what I always said, Dick, a woman's at the bottom of all the mischief.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: You're right, Bill. Why, it's a curious coincidence, but I'm after three o' the soft sex myself.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: What will Mrs. Watchitt say?

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Never you mind Mrs. Watchitt, Bill; this 'ere's business a private and confidential matter; none of your swindling games. There's two young ladies, a Miss Edith Belton, daughter o' the advertiser, and a Miss Amy Leigh, neice o' the same, has run away from home, accompanied by a lady's maid name o' Mary Crick.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: I 'pose you got a description.

sp.speaker: Wacth.
sp.p: O' course I have. What's the good o' that, it they're half sharp they'll be dressed up as somebody else.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Who was it as they run away with?

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: No one as I know of. There was some family trouble about old Mr. Belton marrying again, and bringing a stepmother into the family.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: And then the stepmother and the daughter has a turn up together. I see.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Just so. They've got an aunt—a Mrs. Franks, a foreigner as married Miss Belton's uncle on the mother's side. She is supposed to be living in Switzerland, and it's likely enough the gals 'ud go to her. I must make my enquiries here.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p:
I have. My Countess and the "Old Beau" ain't
here. I'll be back again in the course of the day. This detective business is a very pleasant one isn't it? lots o'pay—

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: And the public does all the work. If you hear anything to my advantage tip us the office.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: All right, and you do the same by me.

div.stage: Exit Marks at gate, L. C., and off, L.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Hum! I shall make this my headquarters, until I hear from my mate Dark at Dover. Garcon!

div.stage: Pierre comes down quickly from up the stage, R.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Yes, sir.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Ah! you're an Englishman. (aside) So much the better.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Yes, sir. Which noble birthright we may both congratulate ourselves upon. There being so many English at Madame Berliot's, she being a sort of broken Englishwoman herself, that she found it convenient—

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: To get a quick, sharp, handy London waiter like you.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Exactly so, sir.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: A sort of likely young man that knows a good customer again when he sees him.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: My case, sir, to a T. I always remembers afterwards them as remembers me first. I never forgot a name or a face when I were second waiter at Smart's Theatrical Coffee 'Ouse, near Coving Garden, where I were in the 'abit o' meeting a deal o' professional talent. But what o' that, sir? "Hall the world's a stage;" Divine Williams.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Ah, you ought to ha' been on the stage.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Which I tried it in the Provinces. The Hordience were prejudiced again' me. The Hordience was a hass. I returned to waiting and took this 'ere leading business. As the Divine Williams says, "There is a tide in the affairs of man" which, taking it at its flood, led me to Switzerland.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Very full here, I suppose?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Three beds wacant on this side o' the establishment, and one on the other.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: I only want one.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Werry good, sir. Tray bong, as we say on the Continong. (calls) Louis! Enter Louis, L., from hotel. Here! Ici, portay, voo says effay de Mossoo ar numero vang. Toot sweet.

sp.speaker: Louis.
sp.p: Nombair twentee. Ya-as.

div.stage: Exit, Louis, L.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Great thing to know a furrin language, sir.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: But you've plenty of English here?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Full, a'most always.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Has Miss Belton left yet?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Miss, miss—not 'ere, sir.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Oh yes, with her cousin, Miss Leigh, and a maid.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Not 'ere, sir. I never forgets anybody; but you can see the list o' visitors in the hall. "List, list! oh list!" Divine Williams—ahem!

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: (aside) This fellow may have been bribed. I must wait for Dark's letter, and if I could only get some sort of a—

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Key, sir, (giving one) of number twenty. Please to lock the door when you go out, otherwise the manager won't be responsible for your properties.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: I hope it's not very high up.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: The room is somewhat high, but our charge is low. You're not a member o' the Alpine Club, are you, sir?

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: No—why?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Oh, one of our gents, Mr. Stepitt, is, and he says as the ascent of the stairs is werry good practice.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: I'll try them presently. (about to go up, L. C.—observes Lester) Mr. Ferguson seems rather busy?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: R. C. Ferguson! you mean Mr. Lester. He's been stopping in the 'ouse for ever so long.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Ah yes, I recollect. (goes up—coughs as he passes Lester—Lester looks up from his work, they salute one another politely– exit Watchitt, R., at back, out of gate)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Pierre! who's that?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Don't know, Mr. Lester. He ain't wrote his name down yet. "What's in a name?" Divine Williams! Ahem!

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (without, in salle à manger of R. house) Pierre! Pierre!

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: (crosses to L. –imitating) Pierre! it sounds better than John, somehow there's more of a—of a—Jenny—say quaw—a—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (without) Pierre! (enters through window on to verandah, R., followed by the Shoemaker, the Tailor, the Hairdresser, a Female, and another – to them) You 'ave all your order for de gentlemens and ladies dat come to my bâl masquè. If one anyting else, some fancy dress, some boot, I send to you. Exeunt Tradesmen at back, R. and L. (to Pierre) Now, vat for you stand chattering all day like a —vat you call—like a tart.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: A tart, Madame Berliot?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Oui. Vat you say in English—he chatter like a —a pie.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Oh, a magpie! Allow me to say, madame—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Ah, dat will do. You have to arrange for de picnic—de 'amper—de baskets and den for de bâl masquè.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (coming down, L., carrying a small portfolio) So, that's to come off this evening, madame?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p:
(c.) Ah! Bon jour, M'sieu Lestaire. Oui. Everybody
ask me, "Vy not give a bâl—light up ze garden ." I cannot refuse dem. (turns quickly on Pierre) Vat for you listen ven I talk to anybody? (to Lester) So I say it shall be done; and at once. For what is dat of your poet, who write, "Vat is to be done vell must be done quickly ." Eh?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: R. aside She's a hinsulting the memory of the Divine Williams. (about to speak—she turns round upon him sharply) Yes, Madame Berliot. Exit through window, R.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: You speak English very well, madame. (standing by L. table, and arranging his portfolio)

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (with some tinge of sadness in her manner) Oui, avec raison. Ven young, I marry an English merchant—I live in England, tree, four year, at de house of my 'usband. I leave because –(stops herself, takes a pinch of snuff, and resumes quietly) I like not de climate ; he disagree vid me.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (politely) What your husband?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: No; the climate. (takes snuff)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Ah! you're lucky in climate here; we shall have our dance by the light of the moon ; and (L.C.) I've made designs for the costumes of nearly all your guests, they've been copied.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (taking portfolio to table, L.) Ah, dat is kind ; it is not defended den for me to look at de original picture?

div.stage: Enter, from L. window; Alfred Stepitt, in the dress of an Alpine tourist.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: (C.) Morning, Mr. Lester.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Bon jour, Monsieur Stepitt.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Ah, madame! I'm off, you see, to choose a good site for our pic-nic to-day in the mountains. (going up, R.)

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: (without) Alf! Alf! (enter, L.) Not without me; you know 'tis my climbing day, and you promised to take me and Emmy with you.

div.stage: Enter Emmy Skelter, L.; both young ladies are dressed in appropriate Alpine tourist hats, and carry alpenstocks.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: (R.C.) My sister, Mr. Lester, (they bow) is getting up a Female Alpine Club; Miss Skelter (bow) is the leading member.

div.stage: Enter Sir Thomas Mosthyn, with fishing rod, R.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (R.) Wherever Miss Skelter leads, the gentlemen of the old club will dare anything to follow. (goes up, R.)

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: Now do take great care; you'd much better come fishing with me.

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: L. C. Fishing's so slow, Sir Thomas.

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: (C.) So slow, Sir Thomas.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: The gymnastic exercise of la perche is not so good as that of la pole.

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: Oh, Alf! (laughs)

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: Oh, Mr. Stepitt! (titters)

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Rather good, that; we prefer to hook it—over the mountain passes—hook it—see.

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: Oh, Alf! (laughs)

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: Oh, Mr. Stepitt. (titter)

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: Hook—perch—ha, ha! (as if puzzled)

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: So at present, Sir Thomas, we won't go out with the "Judicious Hooker ." Shall go up the Jungfrau one of these days—fifteen thousand eight hundred and sixty-four feet above the level of the sea—hang on by your eyelids—one slip, all up with you, I mean all down. Write to the Times—yarn at the club when you get back. Come along, girls. (they run up, R.)

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (at L. table) Mademoiselles, remember dat we wait for you to be our guides ; and don't fatigue yourselves before the bâl.

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: (L. C.) We've copied the pretty sketches for our dresses.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: M'sieu Lester did dem; he 'ave done some more.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: (hurriedly looking at portfolio) Ha! yes, capital! Very like Lady Mosthyn, that—so's that. Lester at L. table

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: Yes, so's this.

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: They are very like Lady Mosthyn. (returning pictures to Madame Berliot)

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: So they are—ha, ha! very funny! Come on, girls, we're going "ober de mountain."

div.stage: Exeunt Alfred, Hetty, and Emmy, L., at back—Lester is about quietly to put pictures back.

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: (R.) Like my wife. Ah! let me see. Madame Berliot hands him two pictures Ah! so it is something.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (L.) Quite accidental. Yes, now you mention it—as you say, there is a something.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (c.) Oui, dere is a something. (Lady Mosthyn appears at window over verandah R., and Lester looks up at her—Madame Berliot catches her eye) A something about de eyes.

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: R. C. Well, yes, there may be; but on looking more closely I don't see it myself. It often happens that what strikes others forcibly, quite escapes one's own notice. (aside) He's hooked.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Mais oui. (takes snuff) And how is miladi, dis morning?

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: But so so. She's so excitable, she will mix herself up in foreign politics, and they fly to her head. (gets, c.) I want her to go fishing, but she won't, and I'm so fond of fishing.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Then she's not going out this morning?

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: Going out! She's stopping at home to read the papers. (Madame Berliot watching R. window) But, dear me, I'm losing all the morning when I ought to be fishing. (aside to Lester) Between you and me, Mr. Lester, I'm glad my wife doesn't come out with me.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Yes, fishing is tedious. (at table, L.)

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: 'Tisn't the fishing, you know, but there are such pretty girls come down to the bathing house on the stream. I've got my old Margate telescope. He, he, he! and I say, its given me such a capital idea for a fancy dress at the bâl. I shall go dressed as—Who was that lucky dog that saw Diana and her nymphs bathing?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Actæon.

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: That's the gentleman. He. he, he! He was changed into a stag you know. Good idea, isn't it. (going) Don't tell any one, or they'll all be copying me. Good morning, Madame Berliot.

div.stage: Exit at back, L., by gate.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: R. C. Oui, Miladi Mosthyn do trouble herself too mosh about de foreign politic. Dere are tings nearer home she might attend to. You are fond of politic too, Monsieur Lester?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (sitting R. of L. table) Yes, Lady Mosthyn and myself have occasionally talked the subject over together, and

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (seriously and gently) Oh! M'sieu Lester, you 'ave stay 'ere a long time. I take—I know not vy—an interest in you. (going to Lester) What I 'ave seen—I 'ave seen. Par-r-rdon, m'sieu, I do not wash you. I know dat when se 'usband go out, de window is a leetle opened. (the window is opened by a concealed hand) Tink, oh! tink vell before you disturb ze 'appiness of a family. I have known it myself.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Madame—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: I am an ole woman now, and may talk to you. I should 'ave been 'appy in my English 'ome, but for some one dat come between us—between 'usband and vife. De love dat by right belonged to me, was given to anoozer. Von day, helas! I discover to exist a leetle shild dat call my 'usband father. (suppresses her emotion and resumes) It was not de climate, Monsieur Lester, dat make me leave England. (takes a pinch of snuff)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: My dear Madame Berliot, you put too serious a construction upon—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Par-r-rdon, M'sieur Lester. I say dis becos I know dat you vill be 'appier for de 'appiness of ozers, and will find an object worzy of your love.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Ah! that's past. (signal at Lady Mosthyn's window; he rises)

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (R. C.) Aha! you would go. (shrugs her shoulders) You colour.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Colour, madame. I'm a painter.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (aside) Comment! How is it dat I shall detain him?

div.stage: Feversham heard without, R., singing "Libiamo."

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (aside) I must go. (aloud) I've left a paint brush indoors.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (taking snuff) Ah!

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: So I'll just put up my portfolio, and (is about to cross to R. house, when Feversham enters by R. window under verandah, still singing and flourishing his pocket handkerchief very near Lester's face)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (up R. C.) Par-r-r-don. Eh—why—Lester!

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (up R.C.) What Feversham (they shake hands heartily)

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (aside) C'est bon. I vill go myself and talk to Madame Mosthyn; he shall not vant his paint brush now.

div.stage: Exit by R. window.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: At your old work I see.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: And you—

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: I'm travelling tutor—a walking passport to polite society. Three volumes of etiquette, bound up in one pocket companion ; master of the ceremonies, teacher of the Queen's English, and instructor of music to his Royal Highness John Grigson, Esq., of Any Hall, Any-where-you-like-to-put-it-shire.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Instructor in music! That I can understand ; let me see: the last time I saw you, two years ago—you have a talent for musical composition, and were studying en amateur.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Now I'm teaching professionally.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (sits L. of L. table) That is to speak in a business-like way for money?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Precisely. To speak in a musical way—note for note, as you say.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: But, my dear boy, two years ago you had a running account at your banker's?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Yes; it ran so confoundedly fast that I very soon lost sight of it altogether.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Dear me, I'm really very sorry; I—

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Don't mention it. (sits on table, L.) I'll tell you all about it with pleasure. We might open the story—the "Romanza" with a little recitative. What a pity it is that we don't ordinarily talk in recitative! Thus you'd exclaim: (singing) "Your story interests me ." (imitates the chord of a violoncello after an ordinary recitative) Then I—(singing again) "Does it indeed! does it indeed! " (chord) "I'll tell you all—list to my tale!" Then you "Proceed, sir! " (imitates the two finishing chords) Now for the Romanza. (imitates a harp accompaniment)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: I'm glad to see you can be merry over your own losses.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p:
Yes; but they're a bad subject for a grand opera. You can't make much out of city or turf speculations. (sits on chair, R. of L. table) I speak as a musician, and repeat the observation as a man. I betted, speculated, took something out of this and that I knew nothing about, invested it in something else of which I knew less. My advisers told me that everything I tried was safe. So they were –safe to fail. I don't remember
an exception. Speaking musically I was nothing more than a flat, accidentally among the sharps ; one morning I found myself without a single note.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Ah, that's a musician's account of the matter.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: I beg your pardon, it was my banker's account of the matter. However, ruined as I was, I still had a voice in my own affairs, and determined to use it. I saw at once that I must fall back on my chest—speaking musically, so I began to give lessons to a young lady, a Miss Amy Leigh, whose charms can only be described by a tenor song, at the beginning of an opera.

div.stage: Enter L. at back during the above, Mary Crick, disguised as a man servant, Edith Belton, as an American gentleman, and Amy Leigh, as his wife. A Porter carries their luggage, and exits by path into the front of house, R.—Edith and Amy pause, and closely observe Lester, and Feversham, attracting each other's attention, at different points of the conversation referring to them.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: And you fell in love with her?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: I own it. She saw it, and so did her aunt.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Did she return your passion?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Who? the aunt?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: No, the girl.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Yes, and so did the aunt with interest. My passion—'gad! it was a joke to her she stigmatized my teaching, as thorough base—musically speaking at least she wasn't musically speaking—she had the voice of a crow, that Mrs. Belton.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Belton! why—

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Ah! of course that's the name of the pretty girl to whom you were engaged two years ago. (hums a tune)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Yes, Edith Belton.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Ah! you ought to be married by now—p'raps you are; allow me to congratulate you!

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: On being still a bachelor. I did love her!

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: And do? (hums a tune)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: I don't know. Perhaps I've found metal more attractive. I was told that she was playing fast and loose with me, and that she was really in love with some one else.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Did you not tax her with the duplicity?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: No; my letters were returned, and—and I gave her up. I have since thought that her friends misrepresented me to her, and mine did the same kind office by her to me.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: One soon gets over those sort of things; and I dare say you've an intrigue or two on here—eh? rum tum mysterious music. (imitates)

div.stage: Exeunt Edith and Amy into house, shown in by Pierre, R.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Talking, of music, I wonder how my pupil's getting on.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Your pupil? (rises—crosses to R.—walks about, watching Lady Mosthyn's window, picking flowers, &c.)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Oh, yes! I haven't told you about him. (lights pipe) Well, when I lost my music lessons and my love at one blow, I felt wretched ; and, while in this state, I called for the police reports—my usual reading when I'm unhappy that sheet of the paper was "in hand," and so I began looking over the advertisements—one caught my eye !—"Neglected Education—Wanted by a young man, whose education has been neglected, a gentleman, as travelling companion and tutor; also to instruct in music and singing, and introduce the advertiser into polite society. Terms: three hundred pounds per annum and everything paid, with an annuity for life."—Need I say I jumped at it.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: And got it!

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: I did! The neglected creature was a good-hearted soul—one Grigson—the son of an anonymous pair of parents ; his father, without ever seeing him, suddenly left him a large fortune, and so the Boots–

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Boots?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Yes! my pupil had been earning a respectable livelihood in that capacity. Well, Boots, on stepping into his father's shoes, determined to be a gentleman, but found himself, as you may imagine, somewhat deficient in the ingenious arts.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Boots wanted polishing!

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Exactly! I'm doing it ; and this morning, as I've got the superintendence of the musical arrangements at the ball here, I've been coaching Mr. Grigson up in a Swiss song, which he's to sing in a Swiss dress.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (without as if practising) "Oh, come, love, with me to the—tullaliety."

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (rising) He can't quite get hold of the Jödel. Hush! don't let him see you laughing at him.

div.stage: Enter Grigson, L., from house.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Tulla—tul—la—li—e—e—ty! I can't get it, you know. I say, Feversham; looky 'ere.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Here! Here!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (C.) Nownochaff. I ain't a making a parlourmentary speech—what I said was this, don't yer know, looky 'ere

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: L. C. And what I said was here—here, not 'ere.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Ho yes. Now mind yer, halways—I mean always —correct me when I slips—I mean slip—which hain't horften —I mean orphan—

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Yes, you are improving.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (seeing Lester) Oo's that cove?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Cove?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p:
Vell—I mean well—'im in the stylish 'at—I mean
hat—no, 'at; I say, which his it? Don't leave a feller floundering about like this, yer know.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Hat, not 'at. A friend of mine, a gentleman of family. Mr. Lester, Mr. Grigson. (they bow)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: 'Ow do you do, sir. Ow—I mean how's your family?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: You're very good, sir, but I have none.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (aside to Feversham) You said he was a man of family.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Pooh! (sits L. of L. table)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: How do you like Switzerland, Mr. Grigson?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Well, hit's a sort of a—I mean it's a— (to Feversham) How do I like Switzerland?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: My friend, Mr. Grigson, can scarcely find words to express his admiration. I dare say, Lester, you've made a number of sketches of places that we've visited. (to Grigson) Mr. Lester's an artist.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: A hartist—I mean a nartist.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: You've ascended the Rigi, of course.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Yes, me and 'im was took up there on mules—'tain't nothink at all. Me an' 'im was called out o' bed at four o'clock in the morning, jolly cold yer know, like a hicicle—I mean a nicicle—we was called by the blowing of a cow's 'orn as was playing one o' the tunes o' which the hanimal died I should say ; and then we went hout—I mean out to see the sun rise. Rise! pooh! 'tain't nothink at all, and if you hain't bin, don't go—hit's a himposition! (the Waiter from L. brings in light wine and soda, Grigson lights a cigar)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Then you were disappointed! (lighting a cigarette)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Disappointed! rather. The performance deserved 'issing and I 'issed it.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Hissed.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Eh? (looking about) What's the matter?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Are you looking for the h you dropped? Hissed, not 'issed.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Now mind you halways—I mean always correct me, 'cos that's our bargain yer know.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (who has gone to verandah, R., aside) I think I hear the rustle of a dress.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Now this is what I calls jolly. I say, you've hordered our dresses for the bal marsky?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Yes, you're to be a merry Swiss boy. (sings) Tulla li, &c.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Ho, gemminy!

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (sitting, R.) And I'll take your likeness.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Will you? (aside) He's a laughin' at me, but I'll give him a good 'un. (aloud) When I wants a pieture I'll go to hartist—I mean a nartist—as can paint. (aside) 'Ad 'im there.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Ah! then you'll come to me. (rises) Yours is a sort of face that can't be taken full.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Eh, why?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (C.) It never could be full, it's so empty. Good morning.

div.stage: (as Lester goes towards R., Lady Mosthyn enters by verandah, they converse together )

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I know he was a laughin' at me.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: You must get accustomed to this light sort of badinage; give and take.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Oh, I can give, you know, but as to taking—

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Yes, that's quite another pair of shoes.

div.stage: Edith and Amy enter from the house, R., and observe Lester and Lady Mosthyn.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Now don't talk about shoes, it ain't kind of you, particular as you know the commercials always used to call me Stockings.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Stockings! why?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Well, 'acos I was under-boots; so don't say it again.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: (R. C.) Ah, Mr. Lester.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (down C.) You are unhappy, Lady Mosthyn.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: The thought of my hard lot in life ; married for family reasons, to an old man, who cares more for fishing than for me.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: 'Tis in your power to change it.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: In my power? how? (casting her eyes down)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: How! (very significantly) If you will but permit me to show you–– (taking her hand)

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Oh, Mr. Lester, I—

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: You will trust me, will you not?

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: I have ceased to trust anybody.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Except one, who could not deceive, because he loves you.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Love! Oh, Mr. Lester, it would have been better for both of us if we had never met.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Say that you will meet me to-night.

div.stage: Enter Madame Berliot by window, R. house.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Hush! (aside) As I wished. (aloud) Madame Berliot is watching us, and will inform my husband.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: They're a going it.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Capital duet for music.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: The dressmaker vish for to speak vid you, Madame Mosthyn. Ah! (takes a pinch of snuff and shakes her head at Lester, then crosses to Feversham—Lester goes up)

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: If my husband returns for the pic-nic before I am able to see you again, I will send you a note, and appoint when we shall––

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Meet?

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Alone.

div.stage: Lester kisses her hand, and Lady Mosthyn exit into house, R. the last words are overheard by Edith and Amy, who are seating themselves at R. table.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: (to Edith—aside) A letter, and a meeting.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: I hear.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Den, Monsieur Feversham, I shall depend upon you for ze direction of ze music at de bal, and for everything dat amuse.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Madame Berliot, I undertake the responsibility of master of the ceremonies, and my friend will assist.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Ah, but you know I came to Swizzleland on business.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Some law business, madame.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: And I was talking of plaisir. (snuff)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Yes; but I must harsk you somethink, mum.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (L.C.)Me? (Feversham imitates a chord)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I hain't a good 'and at a story, but you understand me, I come to Swizzleland to look after a hold lady which it might be you ; no hoffence meant, mum, where none ain't taken.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: So you told me, but that's the point where you always stuck. Sing it. (imitates harp accompaniment)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I can't get on if you do that. You see my mother died before I was born—no—I mean I never 'ad a mother, that is her name was Grigson. Leastways my father's wife was a farrier—I mean a furriner, and she went away, and nobody never 'eard nothink at all about her. You understand, mum?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Eh, bien? (snuff)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Well, where was I? oh, well, when my guv'nor goes off the hooks—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Ze hook?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Translate—dies.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Ah, oui.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Then he leaves all his money to you, that's what you were going to say.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: No, it harn't. But the will says this 'ere to his executors and designs, 'arf to my wife, who's abroad, Mrs. Franks—which was his name if she can be found. Hallo, mum! what's the matter?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: 'Tis nothing; ze weather. Feversham offers her Grigson's chair

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (standing by table, L.) So here we come to look hafter 'er. You don't 'appen to know on such a party do you ?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p:
(recovering herself, c.) I did. A long time ago—twenty year more—a Mrs. Franks, but she say den to me, "І give up all claim to de name dat made me life miserable; I
wish to forget him, and be forgotten." Dat is vat she say to me twenty year ago. I 'ave not heard of Mrs. Franks since. (crosses to R.—Feversham and Grigson converse closely)

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (to Amy) Strange! I must ask her a question. (aloud, rising, and assuming the American twang, R. C.) I guess vou'll excuse the curiosity, but was the sister of that Mrs. Franks you spoke of the first wife of a Mr. Belton?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (down C., with emotion) De first wife! Grand Dieu ! Den she too is dead?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (forgetting her assumed manner) Yes. (Amy pulls her sleeve—Edith resumes) Oh! yes. You kinder knew her and Mrs. Franks too?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Well, I knew dem both. No one 'ave heard of Mrs. Franks for many a long year.I tink now dat she must be dead too.

div.stage: Exit slowly into house, R.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (to Feversham) Well, if she's out o' the way that's all right you know, and I shan't 'ave to give up 'arf o' the tin.

div.stage: Enter Pierre, with biscuits and wine, which he places on R. table.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: (to Grigson) Your dresses for the bal marsky have come, sir. You'll see 'em in there, sir. (pointing L., and crossing)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: We'll go and see them. (rises and crosses to L.)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (coming down, R. C., impatiently, and looking up at Mrs. Mosthyn's window) No letter—no sign of any sort. I suppose her husband has returned. (he sits with his hat in his hand)

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: (behind table, L.) One o' the costumes is a Swish boy, like that picture o' yourn, Mr. Lester.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Ah! that was a fancy sketch. I wish I could get an original for a model. The genus have vanished from the land. (aside) I'll try if I can see her at the front window.

div.stage: Goes up and off at back, leaving his hat on table up R.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (R. of R. table, to Amy) He shall have the model.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: (L. of R. table, to Edith) How? (Edith motions her to silence)

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p:
(going to L. house) The tailor here is werry reasonable; but what I says is :—
Costly thy 'abit as thy purse can buy,
But rich—not gaudy." Divine Williams. Hem! This way!

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p:
(rising—getting to L. C.) Come! (imitating chord—sings)
Oh! come up with me to the top o' the hill,
And I'll show the place I was born in.
Tulla-li-e-ty. Exit, L., into house.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Tulla-li-e-ty! Oh! I can't do it.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p:
So, Edith, the only chance of finding our aunt has
gone. Madame Berliot said she hadn't been heard of for years. I wonder what those gentlemen wanted with her? Do you think we're discovered?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Amy, you really are frightened at anything and by anybody.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: I can't help thinking that it would have been much better to stay at home, instead of coming out like Rosalind and Celia.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: What! and submit to stepmother, who treated both of us cruelly?

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: Well, but our money's getting less and less. (rises) What shall we do?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (rises) We'll think of that when it's gone. We may find Mrs. Franks, our aunt, before that, and at all events we have now the opportunity of discovering whether our lovers are true.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: (down R.C.) I'm afraid it doesn't look like it. Didn't you hear Mr. Feversham say, as we came in. "One soon gets over this sort of things ." He was talking of his love for me.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (down R.) Yes, and I heard Edward Lesteranswer, that he didn't know whether he was still in love with me or not ." I must decide him upon that point.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: But just now he made an appointment with that Lady Mosthyn.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: No, she made an appointment with him ; but I'll soon take him away from her, that is, if I find he's worth the taking.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: But how will you manage?

div.stage: Enter Mary Crick, R.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: That's my secret ; only don't be surprised if I leave you alone for a few hours.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (down R., after looking cautiously round) The waiter mentioned promiscuously that there's been a gent enquiring for Miss Belton and Miss Leigh.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: I know we shall be found out. (drops her pocket handkerchief in attempting nervously to put it in her pocket)

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Nonsense. Our names are down in the visitor's book as Colonel and Mrs. Smart, of New York. What sort of person was this?

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: I don't know, miss—I mean Colonel. But I can't help fancying that he's a police—not a riggilar Blue, but a Defective.

div.stage: Enter Grigson, carrying a dress; also Feversham, L.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: But I can't wear it; it's several sizes too small. I can't be a merry Swiss boy of seven years old.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Well, get another. (looking at Amy whose back is turned to them) I'll bet that's a pretty girl.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (aside to Amy) A Swiss boy's dress.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: She's helegant.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: They're the Americans who came this morning. Now, if you want to make your name for gallantry, pick up that handkerchief, and offer it to her.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Oh, but I say, yer know.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (to Amy) De cautious, and don't forget that you're now Mrs. Colonel Smart, of New York.

div.stage: Grigson, L. C., has advanced timidly, and picks up handkerchief—Mary, R. by R. table, who has not perceived the action, accidentally coughs—Amy, R. C. up, turns—Feversham, L. down, starts on seeing her—Grigson in a frightened manner hides the handkerchief behind him, and stands with his eyes fixed on Mary—Edith, R. C. down, motions Amy to go –exit Amy into house, R.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (aside to Edith) Oh, miss, it's the Boots!

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Boots!

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (as before) —As used to keep company with me at the Commercial.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (to her) Pshaw! (crosses to Grigson) I beg your pardon; do you wish to part with that dress?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (still eyeing Mary) Well, you know, I can't wear it; it's too small.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: It's just what I want for my little boy. Take the costume, Nat. (to Mary, who takes it off table, L.) Tell your snip to chalk that up to me—Colonel Smart, of New York, and consider it a deal. Good morning, sir. Exit into house, R.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (not recovered from his astonishment) Yes, but—

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (C., in his ear) Boots!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Coming ––(looks round)

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (touching her hat impudently) Good morning, sir. Exit carrying the Swiss dress into house, R.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Am I dreaming?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: The very remark I was going to make myself. Am I snoring? No—is this a chair? it is, and a 'at, the hartist's 'at—it is. (puts it on)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (L.) Married to an American! and yet it must have been she.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (L.) It must ha' been.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (turning sharply on him) Do you know her?

div.stage: Enter Watchitt at back, L.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Do I? the only girl I ever loved!

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: You! Amy Leigh!

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: (aside) He! Amy Leigh! Hallo!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: No; Mary Crick.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: (aside) Ha!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p:
As I was saying, the only girl I ever –(about
to sneeze) I ever – dear me! (pulls out Amy's pocket handkerchief—Feversham takes it)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: It is. (looks at mark) Amy Leigh! (crosses to R., as he does so, Grigson takes it from him again)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Don't you take it or I shall get into a row.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: (aside) Oho!

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (aside) I must try to see her. Exit into house, R.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: That must ha' been Mary Crick's brother. Bother the handkercher! I can't give it to the lady; I'll leave it 'ere, (places it on table, L.) and she'll come and find it. (going towards house, stops by entrance to verandah)

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: (taking up handkerchief—aside) Amy Leigh! if it is, she's in a precious good "get up." I must keep my eyes open. (retires)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (meditating) Some one said "Boots" distinctly.

div.stage: Lady Mosthyn opens the window above, R.—Sir Thomas appears by her.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: (to Sir Thomas) Don't show yourself. He's below; I see his hat.

sp.speaker: Sir Thomas.
sp.p: (chuckling) The fish is caught! (lets down a note by a string– it bobs against Grigson's nose)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Confound the flies! Halloa—a note!

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: (down L., catching sight of La Comtessa at the window) The "Old Beau" and Countess, by Jove! I must tip Marks the office at once. Exit at back—the window closes.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: What's this? "Dearest"! "Oh my! Dearest, meet me during the pigs—p-i-c-n-i-c!" Ho! pic-nie! "And settle all for to-night ." Oh! my figure's caught somebody's heye. I wonder 'oo hit is.

div.stage: Enter Lester, hurriedly, at back, R., and comes down.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Not a single word or a message. Can she have changed her mind? (looking up at window, then goes to chair up R.) I could have sworn I left my hat here!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (R.) The hartist. Lost anything?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Yes; my hat.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: R. C. An 'at, with an 'ed in it? (taking it off)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (snatching it away angrily) What do you mean, sir, by –– (threatening)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (R. C., retreating, but showing fight) I say, come none o' that, or else I'll ––

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (L. C.) Eh? Do you want to black my eyes or my boots. (both in pugilistic attitude)

div.stage: Enter Pierre, L., and Waiters—Feversham, R., followed by Lady Mosthyn, Alfred, Emmy, and Hetty, L., at back.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (c.) Chord. (imitates) Combatants parted by the watch. (hums air from the Huguenots)

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: L. C. We've selected a splendid place for a pic-nic.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Now, then, form in procession. Pierre, send on the waiters with the hampers. Pierre crosses to R., and re-crosses to L. at back, with Waiters

div.stage: Guests come on, R. and L., at back.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: (L. c.) There's rather a steep ascent; any lady that's not a good climber will want a mule or a donkey.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (R. C.) Don't let's stop for that.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (R.) You won't want one, sir—this gentleman's going. (all laugh)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Now then, band first. Now where's my chorus? here.

div.stage: March. Watchitt has re-entered, and is observing Grigson closely—Emmy takes Alfred's arm—Grigson is about to offer his to Amy, when Sir Thomas Mosthyn enters and takes her arm—Grigson offers to Hetty, but Feversham gives her his arm—the last couple are Lester and Lady Mosthyn—Feversham directs the band—they move off—Madame Berliot appears, waving her hand to them from the window above, R.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (at verandah, R., through the music, as the last couples are going up stage) Again together! (to Mary) Where?

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (by whom stands a Swiss Guide) To Giessbach Falls ; the guide will take us there by a short cut.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Then quick, quick—the dress!

div.stage: (Music getting fainter and fainter — Tableau — Lady Mosthyn and Lester together at back—Waiters, L. C., watching the party off—Mary, R., with one foot in the window as if going into the house—Edith, R., standing to take a last glimpse of Lester –the Guide, R. C., waiting for them—Madame Berliot watching from window above )

div.head: Act II.
Scene First.—A Mountain Pass in the neighbourhood of Giessbach Falls.
div.stage: Enter L., Pierre, Waiter, and a couple of Porters carrying provisions—Pierre has a large hamper strapped on his back.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p:
If this ain't hot work, I'm blowed—I'm blowed as it is. I think I might wenture on some refreshment out o' this 'ere basket. (suddenly to Porters) We don't stop here, you know. Nong restez ici, nong, montay! Montay! ever so much higher! Daypeshkey vous! Alley toot sweet!
Exeunt Porters, R.
That's it (releases himself from his burthen and sits on it) "Oh! that this too too solid flesh wouldn't melt ."—Divine Williams. Ahem! I think as I might get a modicum of owe de wee.
Kneels, R., and tries to undo basket—Enter Mary Crick, L. Here's retribution! I did it up tight myself, and ain't got a knife.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: Can I help you, Mr. Pierre? Oh! I'm ashamed on you, I am. You're old enough to know better.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Old enough?

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: I didn't say how old.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Now, look here, young two penn'orth o' halfpence, I ain't a going—I say I ain't a going—

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: Ain't yer, I wish you were.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: I ain't going to stand any o' this!

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: Oh! then what are you going to stand? Give it a name.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: (sitting) None o' your imperence. Why you goes a hordering the waiters about and talking to me, as if you were the hemperor o' the place.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: Emperor o' the place, why not? You're a Pierre of the realm. Now then, come! (crosses to R.)

div.stage: Enter L., Two Guides followed by Grigson, with an alpenstock.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I say, don't go at such a confounded pace. Yer know I hain't a mounting goat, or a young gazelle to glad yer with it's bright blue heye—I mean eye.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p:
L., aside I'll wait till they've moved on, and then for my owe de wee, and then return : "
To what base uses may we return, Horatio," Divine Williams.

div.stage: Exit, L.
Exit Pierre—Guides retire, R.—Mary is sitting on hamper.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Ah! here is a seat, thank goodness. (politely) If you'll allow me. Why, (she turns suddenly) is your name—um—um—

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: No; nothing of the sort. I've got a message for you, Mr. Grigson.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: A message?

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: Yes. There's a young lady as you used to keep company with a—going to be at the bâl masque to-night.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: And her name is—um—um.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: No, it isn't, sir; nothing of the sort. Good morning, sir.

div.stage: Touches her hat, and exit, R.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Well, I've known a many queer goes, but of all goes this 'ere is the— Enter Watchitt, closely observing him, L. I say of all queer goes this is the ––Now, what on earth does that fellow want, I wonder?

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: (aside) I'll go to the point at once. (aloud) Now, miss!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Miss!

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: It won't do any longer. Capital get up, Miss Amy!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Hamy! (aside) He's mad.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Why don't you resume your natural crinoline and petticoats?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (after looking at him aside) The mountain hair's got into 'is 'ead.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Let me give you a wrinkle. When you want to remain unknown don't carry handkerchiefs about with your name on 'em—"Miss Amy Leigh !" (shows pocket handkerchief) Ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Ha, ha! you sly dog! (suddenly) Stop, hi see hit hall. Mary, appears, R., listening, behind Guide. That's not mine.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Whose then? (suspiciously)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Why I got that from—from a—

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: Hush! (disappears)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (startled, turns to Guide) I wish you wouldn't do that.

sp.speaker: Guide.
sp.p: (R.) Mais, m'sieu, me not speak at all.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Well then don't make them 'orrid noises. Hunhedicated furriners!

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Well, this handkerchief—

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Hit belongs to a lady at the 'otel. She'll be at the marsky ball this hevening, and hi'll p'int her out.

sp.speaker: Watch.
sp.p: Very good; then I'll keep my eye on you. (going, turns) I say, didn't you get a letter from some female party—eh? You're not bound to commit yourself you know that's the law—only when the little meeting does come off, I shan't be a hundred miles away. (aside) This is one o' the Countess's flats. I shouldn't wonder if Marks don't nab the pair on 'em now. Exit, L.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Little meeting,! Lady, as I know on, at the Masky Ball.! Amy Leigh—

sp.speaker: Guide.
sp.p: (crossing to L.) If Monsieur come oop, von steep short way, I put a rope round him, and he no fall, ven he go first. (a guide R., behind Grigson, ties rope round his waist—exeunt Guides, L. and R.)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Here! hi! this his interfering with the liberty of the subject!

div.stage: Re-enter Mary cautiously, R., crosses to him.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: You didn't say from whom you got that handkerchief.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: No; 'cos hi didn't know.

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: You mustn't know; if you're asked again, refuse to tell—'sh! we're watched. Exit quickly, L.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Yes, but–– (runs after her—rope pulls him back) Oh, bother! hif this 'ere his the way they goes hup mountings, hi've done with it. (takes off rope and ties it on hamper) Hi'll get hup in my own way. Tulla-hity! tullality! Exit, R.

div.stage: Enter Pierre, with knife, L.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Nobody here? now then to cut the string. Take a little owe de wee. Now for the cut. (kneels down—rope falls—he falls flat—exit hamper) Here—cut! "This his the unkindest cut of hall ." Divine Williams!

div.stage: Exit Pierre, R.

div.head: Scene Second.—Tableau Third.—Wooded Height, near the Giessbach Falls; small cascades.—Music.
div.stage: Lester, Lady Mosthyn, Amy, Feversham, Hetty, Emmy, Alf Stepitt, Mary Crick, Pierre, &c.; Guides, Waiters discovered grouped about in different attitudes—the Waiters and Porters as if having just laid down plates and knives and forks for a pic-nic—hampers open –the large hamper brought in by Pierre in Scene 2 has just been undone by him.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: This is indeed romantic.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Delightful!

sp.speaker: Ladies and Gentlemen.
sp.p: Charming!

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Yes, I think I selected the place pretty well.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Good opening scene for an opera—chorus by everybody—orchestra playing—

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Let me play a knife and fork.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Where's Grigson?

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: (looking through glass) Here he comes, tumbling up.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: It looks a good deal more like tumbling down. Hi, hi—yeo—ho!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (feebly without) Yeo—ho!

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Now then put on the steam.

div.stage: Enter on rock, R., Grigson, with coat much torn.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Oh lor'! I'm done.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Why your pole's broken?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (feeling his head) Is it? Oh! I thought it was.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: He means your Alpenstock.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Yes, that broke as hi was trying to cross a waccwum. It came in half and I went into the hole.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: You can tell Mr. Lester of what you noticed on your road; there were many fine points.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Yes, there were a great many fine points and werry rocky ones too. (painfully)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (L. C.) Ah! they're not much in your way.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I beg your parding, they wos very much in my way; I either sat or fell upon every one on 'em.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (R. C.) Now then, we'll sit down and help ourselves. Pierre and you fellows be off, you've got your eatables lower down. Quick—hurried music.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p:
"
Stand not upon the horder of your going, but go." Divine Williams!

div.stage: Exit Pierre, &c.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (R. of hamper, who during the above has seated himself by the hamper and has selected a pie upon which he is just going to operate) This is comfortable.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Now for the food, Grigson. (Grigson takes no notice)

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: (L. of R. bank) Oh, Lady Mosthyn, look. (directing her attention to Grigson)

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: "Little Jack Horner sat in a corner ." (all laugh—they are now seating themselves as for the pic-nic)

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: (sarcastically) Perhaps Mr. Grigson is rather tired.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Oh no, he only wants a little brushing up. (imitating boot cleaning)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (aside) Hi should like to pitch hinto that 'ere painter. Now I ain't got no fork. (gets up and kneels by basket)

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: (while Feversham removes Grigson'S pie) Some bread please, Mr. Grigson.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Bread, Grigson.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: Bread.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Bread.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Bread.

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: Bread.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Bread.

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: Bread.

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: And bread here, Mr. Grigson.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Um! (makes a dive into the hamper, hands bread, and is just turning away, when)

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: Knives please, Mr. Grigson.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Knives, Grigson.

div.stage: (he dives again—all ask for knives as before, ending with Sir Thomas, who says, "And knives here, Grigson" )

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: Mustard please, Mr. Grigson.

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: Oh, yes, Mr. Grigson, and salt.

div.stage: (as before—adding "Pepper, salt, mustard, vinegar !" &c. )

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I say, I'm getting a headache, I really can't—

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Any chicken there, old fellow?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (surlily) Don't know.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Then look, look. Grigson dives

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Thank you, some chicken, Mr. Grigson.

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: And you may cut a bit for me too.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Now for a morsel. (helps himself quickly)

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: And me too. Alfred takes away Grigson'S plate and passes it Emmy

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I say—

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: For a lady.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: I'm afraid we're giving you a great deal of trouble?

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Well

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (aside) Must say no, and smile.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (grins hideously) Oh, no, no!

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Some ham, please.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: What a sketch you can make of this, Lester.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (going to hamper at back, gives a bottle to Alfred, two to Sir Thomas, who gives one to Lester) Here's something better worth drawing.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Eh?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: A cork. (handing champagne bottles)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (seeing bottles pointed in his direction) Hi! take care!

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.l: "Uprouse ye, then, my merry, merry men,

sp.speaker: All.
sp.l: "Uprouse ye, then, my merry, merry men,

div.stage: (pop—the Four Gentlemen open the four bottles at once )

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: Glasses, Mr. Grigson. (all say "Glasses")

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Glasses. (fills Grigson's glass and hands it to Amy)

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: This won't do, you know, Hi can't get nothink at all.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: My dear Mr. Grigson, you really think of nothing but eating and drinking. (drinks)

sp.speaker: Amy.
sp.p: No soul for the beauties of nature.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: No appreciation of the picturesque.

sp.speaker: Hetty.
sp.p: No ideality. (eats)

sp.speaker: Emmy.
sp.p: No pure love for the country.

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: No manliness of tone.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Oh, Grigson! when will you learn?

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: Oh, Mr. Grigson!

p.l: Oh, Mr. Grigson, unfortunate young Grigson!
Oh, Mr. Grigson, unfortunate young Grigson!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Now looky 'ere

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Here.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Well, here all on you; Hi don't care for chaff, that tain't nothink at all, and hif the ladies likes hit, Hi'm agreeable.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Question.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Order.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Which I can't horder—there not bein' no waiter in the room—but hi ain't a-going to be done out o' my lawful wittles, not for nobody.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p:
(on bank, R.) Eat, eat—don't talk. (lighting a cigarette)
Re-enter Pierre and Mary, L. Don't you see they're waiting to pack up the hamper again.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: I can wait, sir, till the gentleman's quite done. (commences putting up the things)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: And now for some music. First the time and place. Halt of the caravans. Aged chieftains reposing require something to cheer them. I'm the aged chieftain! Strike the light guitar. Now then, Hetty! Pierre, fetch one of them fellows with the instrument. Guide brings in a lady's guitar—Song—Duett, Emmy And Hetty

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Now, Grigson, give us a song.

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: Give us a song.

sp.speaker: Sir Thom.
sp.p: (suddenly up) Grigson, a song. Grigson begins

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: No excuses about a cold. That little sentimental thing I taught you—"Ever of thee." I'll do the accompaniment.

div.stage: Grigson rises, tries, and fails—at the conclusion all applaud—in bowing his thanks, he backs, and falls into hamper
Re-enter Guides at back, L.

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: (L. C.) Come, let us make the best of our time; we've a pretty stiff bit of climbing before us.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (in hamper, L.) I think I'll stop here, and ––

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: No you won't. Now then. Here's a fresh pole for you.

div.stage: (they are all moving off, when a few notes of a shepherd's pipe are heard without—they pause—Edith sings without—Lester's attention is arrested )

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Hi say, hain't that—

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: a la Jullien Hsssssh!

div.stage: Edith sings, and finishing the last line appears, slowly wending her way down the rocks at back, dressed as a Swiss peasant—Tableau

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Decidedly operatic. Comes down to music. (imitates)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: The very model I want. (kindly) Come here, my boy. (regards him curiously) Strange! (prepares his drawing materials)

sp.speaker: Alfred.
sp.p: Come, Lady Mosthyn, we'd better not interrupt the artist in his studies.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: I am quite ready. (aside to Lester)

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (whose arm Amy has taken) We shall find you here on our return. (to Guides) Now show the way.

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: I will return alone.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p:
"
Lead on! I follow!" —Divine Williams. 'Amlet.

div.stage: Music—Exeunt up the rocks, R. and L.—Lester arranges his sketch book and seat—Edith stands quietly before him.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p:
R.—aside It certainly is a most extraordinary resemblance. Where's my pencil? (aloud) Just stand about where you are that'll do. (aside) He seems frightened.
(aloud) Have you ever had your picture taken? (sits) A little to the right.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (L. C.) I am afraid you find me very awkward, sir.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Awkward! (aside) There's a touch of nature—the lad's grace itself. (aloud) Now. (hums a tune while beginning to sketch)

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (aside) Does he even think of me? (sadly)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (aside– looking up) There's a depression about him that'll never do. I want some light—energy of some sort. (aloud) Have you lived here all your life?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: No, sir. My home is far from here. But I had a cruel stepmother, and so I ran away to others, who I thought loved me—loved me! But–– (sobs and tries to repress her emotion)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Eh! crying?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (looking up and smiling through her tears) Forgive me, sir, I—I—– (with great feeling)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Stop—don't move! That's the expression—admirable! You couldn't have posed yourself better if you'd studied all your life. (Edith sighs—Lester continuing his work) And so you found no one to care for you, or to love you—love you? Ha, ha! You're not yet old enough to know much about love.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Do you love, sir?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: I! (laughs slightly and continues work)

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Yes, that handsome lady who spoke to you just now.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (aside) Hallo! (cutting a pencil) This lad doesn't require much sharpening. (draws) And what do you know about handsome ladies?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Perhaps you're going to be married to her, sir?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (good humouredly) Oh no, she is married. (hums a tune)

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: And you don't love her then?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Love her! (aside) This is an amusing fellow. (aloud) Love her! no; absurd!

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (joyfully) Not! Oh (springing towards him)

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Stop—stop! don't move a step forthe world! I've got it to the life! That's something quite new. Charming!

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (simply) Have you never loved any one?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Never! (pauses thoughtfully) Yes; one; but, ah, you can't understand what I mean.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: I think I can, sir.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (aside) He's getting melancholy again; I must interest him. (aloud) What was I saying?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: You were talking of the lady with whom you were in love.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p:
Ah! (aside) That's clever. (aloud) Well, (drawing) once upon a time there was an artist, who fell in love with a
beautiful young English girl; he was quite mad about her; his head was turned. I want yours a little more this way.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (standing in position) Well.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (aside) He's brightening up. (aloud) Well, there was a cruel stepmother like yours, and she prevailed upon her weak old husband to keep the lovers apart. Don't move! And they were prevented from receiving one another's letters, and then people who pretended to be friends tried to make each believe that the other was false. Do you understand? And they said that the painter was devoted to another mistress and so he was.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: He was?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Yes; his Art! An imperious mistress 'tis true, admitting no rival. And yet when an artist does love, what a passion is it ! the beloved form is always in his heart at his hand—in his thoughts—absent, yet, breathing life-like from the canvas ; she adds a new pleasure to his work—she is his life—a sweet embodiment of his glorious art!

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: Yes, yes.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: And then—tut, tut, tut! What an ass I am; you can't possibly understand this.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: I do; for I love Lester turns painting.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (aside) I feel in a very romantic humour. I've half a mind to play Cimabue to this young Giotto. (aloud) Would you like to come and study with me? (putting up his sketch book, &c.)

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: With you?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: Yes; live with me and be my pupil. Come, you won't get a better offer than that. (rises, and is about to take her hand, when Lady Mosthyn enters quickly on bridge and down, R.)

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Edward! (he goes to her) You told me that you did not receive my letter from the window this morning. To whom do you think I gave it? To that vulgar Mr. Grigson.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (R. C.) But there was no signature?

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: (R.) Nothing: he does not know who sent it.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: I've got it—we'll play him a trick. Here; (to Edith) what is your name?

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (L. C., puzzled) Name—oh—Antoine!

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: My new pupil, Lady Mosthyn, and a great admirer of yours. I can tell you, Antoine, you shall dress up as a girl to-night at the bâl masquè, and we'll have some fun with Grigson. Don't forget, Antoine. Edith goes up, L. C. Lady Mosthyn—Adela—we are now alone, say that you will meet me to-night. Why stay here—why delay our happiness any longer?

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Edward, I may trust you! Then during the bâl masqué at twelve o'clock to-night, at the rose-tree arbour in the garden.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: I will take care that a carriage is ready at the gate; and then—

sp.speaker: Lady M.
sp.p: Then I am yours. (about to fall on his shoulder)

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: (C.) Ah! (faints—Lester runs to her—Music)

div.stage: Enter above, R. and L., Alfred, Feversham, Amy, Hetty, Emmy, &c. &c.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: The picture?

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: (C.) Is finished. Water! water! Amy and Mary run to her assistance

div.stage: Tableau as Scene closes.

div.head: Scene Third.—A Room in Madame Berliot's Hotel.
div.stage: Enter Madame Berliot, followed by Pierre, L.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Dat is strange! You say a gentleman comes to ask you for young gals dat ron avay from England.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Oui, Madame Berliot, he mentioned as they'd run away to their aunt, who is supposed to be hereabouts.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Their aunt?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: A, let's see. What was the name. What's in a name?—Divine Williams.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Villiams—a Mrs. Villiams.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: (aside) 'Ow huncommon hignorant. (aloud) No the name was Mrs. Franks.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: (c., aside) Den dey ask me dat dis morning. What means it to hear dis wretched name again? Bah! (aloud) It is as nothing. (snuffs energetically)

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: (R. C., sneezing) Isn't it?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Is Sir Thomas Mosthyn returned from fishing?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: No, mum, silly old gent, he never gets a bite.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: He vill get more dan he likes, some day.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p:
Just exactly what I was observing, Madame Berliot, for, says I, he's out all day, while Lady Mosthyn is, to say the least of it, a-going on in such a way with a certain party as 'ud make the werry mildest Christian jealous. "
Beware of jealousy; it is a green-eyed monster"—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Green eye—vat dat?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Divine Williams, mum, as usual.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: So you talk of dis vid your companion? My 'otel vill be de scandal of Interlachen.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Yes, 'um, there's a good deal said one way or another about that Mrs. Colonel Smart—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Vat of her?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Oh! nothing, except as I see Mr. Feversham a kissin' 'er 'and like mad, and then when I gave a little cough, she gave a little scream, and he turns round and says, as cool as new-born cucumbers, "We're a rehearsing a part for the bal marskey," (aside) "and here's five francs not to tell," so I says nothing about that.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: It was de part of de lover, dat he take.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: I shouldn't ha' mind taking that un.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Vat you say? I shall discharge you from my respectable hotel if you come dus to talk to me like a Don Juan.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: A Don Juan, madame?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Vat for you defend such conduct as dat of Mr. Feversham?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: I defend him, Madame B.! no, no, no, no, I merely said as I shouldn't mind taking his part.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Ah! den if you will not be a Don Juan, you shall assist me in my trouble. Dese two girls dat have run away in disguise– I ask your advice—vat shall I do?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Well, Madame Berliot, I speak from experience ; and, as they're in disguise, of course you don't know them?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: I! vat for den should I ask you?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Ah! precisely, madame ; then, as I said before, as they're in disguise, you'd better begin by—by—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: By—by—

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: I speak, Madame Berliot, as an adviser of the crown —your crown. "Uneasy lies the head that wears a crown," although it strikes me it would be uneasier without it—Divine Williams.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Au diable vid your Divine Villiams; I tink you take some ob de vine vidout de Villiams. Vat is Villiams to me?

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: "Or he to Hecuba?"—beg pardon, mum ; but as I was saying, regarding these two parties in disguise, the best way to begin is by finding 'em out.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Ah! you are no better as von jackass; you can go.

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Thankye, Madame Berliot. "Oh, that he were here to write me down an ass."—Divine Williams. Yes, Madame Berliot—toot sweet. Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Vat shall I do? (snuff) Mrs. Franks ! ave dat name not been forgot for so many years dat it come again now ? dat name of wretchedness, gone wid de past. Dese foolish girls, too, dat run from deir home, I cannot leave dem alone in de world. Ebery ting go himself wrong at de same moment.

div.stage: Enter Marks, L.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Ah, Madame Berliot, preparing yourself for the grand fête of the evening by a little calm reflection? May I trespass upon you for a pinch?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Avec tout plaisir, M'sieur. (they snuff)

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: An excellent beginning to a conversation is snuff, and in a lady so aristocratic. By-the-way, can you spare me a few moments for a word or two?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Certainment, oui.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p:
Your establishment is very highly patronized! I
hear you've got some friends of mine staying here– the Duke and Duchess of Mountstreet.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: No, m'sieu!

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Ah; then they're coming! Sir Thomas Mosthyn has probably left—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: No, he stop here now.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Within?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Not at dis moment.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Nor Lady Mosthyn?

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Both out all day! He go vun vay to fish—she go —another vay; I know not vere! (snuff)

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: (aside) I've got 'em! (aloud) I see you have the respectable character of your house at heart ; allow me another pinch—thank you– and I judge by your manner that Lady Mosthyn—(Madame B. shrugs her shoulders) Ah, precisely! Madame Berliot, you are a woman of the world ; and I shall therefore have no scruple in forewarning you in this matter.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: I do not understand.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: You shall, ma'am, in one minute. A scandalous story about the Berliot Hotel et Pension would ruin it.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Ah! (shrugs her shoulders, and snuffs)

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Suppose now that there was going to be—allow me —(pinch) an elopement—say, for instance, of ––

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Lady Mosthyn and M'sieu Lestaire! De advice I give dat young man! Oh, dat Sir Mosthyn, he is always out fishing.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Pardon me, ma'am; having your interest at heart, I would say, keep quiet, or else a premature disturbance will be made –the guilty parties be on their guard—and people will say that Madame Berliot is too fond of mischief-making.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: I! I?

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: They will talk.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Then what shall I do?

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Allow me! Previous to stepping into the carriage, the unhappy couple will meet at the Rose Arbour, at twelve to-night, in your garden.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: My garden? Dieu!

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Sir Thomas appears weak and silly. (aside) Cunning old rascal!

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Ver' silly! he is out fishing all de day.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Everyone will wear a disguise—lead him down at the time to that arbour—say a lady wants to speak to him—

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: And show him to his own wife. Ah! 'tis good! (snuffs)

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: They will then go off together and all scandal be prevented.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: A t'ousand t'anks, M'sieu; and I vill tell him—

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p:
Not a word about me. Delicacy, my dear madam,
delicacy, don't let the old gentleman suppose that this affair is known beyond yourself on any account.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: I see, von must be delicate. But I dread ze moment he find her out he vill be transported vid rage.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: (aside) When we find him out he'll be "transported" with justice. (aloud) Good evening, madame. (aside) I shall now catch my two birds in' one net. Exit, L.

sp.speaker: Mad. B.
sp.p: Au revoir, m'sieu. Vat a fool old men is to go out fishing. Ah! (snuffs and exit, .)

div.head: Scene Fourth. Tableau Fifth.—The Grand Bâl Masqué in the Gardens of the Hotel.

View at back of river up and down which the boats, each bearing lights, are swiftly gliding—the stone balustrade at back opens on to a landing place—in distance is seen the opposite bank, with wooded sides, and in perspective the snow mountains—the gardens are lighted with little lamps hanging from the trees the rose arbour with concealed door, R. —paths leading R. 1 E., R. 2 E., L. 1 E., L. 2 E., &c., &c.
div.stage: The Scene opens upon a tableau—the Guests just about to commence a dance—in small parties—some lounging, some being assisted to refreshment by Pierre and Waiters—all in costume, or domino and mask—flourish—dance—during the dance the refrain of the boat chorus, "Homeward we Row," is heard at intervals a party consisting of Feversham, Amy, and Grigson, fantastically disguised as Neptune, land from boat at back and come down.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: (down, C.) There ain't such a nobby dress 'ere—I mean here—this 'ere –

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: (L. C.) Here!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: This here night—all night.

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Capital notion that of mine for you—Neptune.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Who's him?

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: God of the sea! "The sea— the sea!" Rules the waves.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I thought that was honly Britannia as is on the halfpennies. If he's always on the water, what's the good o' this 'ere toasting fork.

div.stage: Mary comes down dressed as a sea nymph

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: We've provided you with an Amphitrite.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Hallo! Why it's ––Music

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Amphitrite! Come and dance in the quadrille.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: I say, miss, will you make up a figure?

sp.speaker: Mary.
sp.p: (R. C.) Make up a figure, indeed! I've got one quite good enough of my own.

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: It's her I know it—it's ––

sp.speaker: Fever.
sp.p: Amphitrite!

sp.speaker: Grigson.
sp.p: Oh, well! (aside) It's her anyhow. (aloud) Come along, Appetitey.

div.stage: (figure of Parisian quadrille—finish they are just going to recommence, and Grigson is expressing in pantomime to his partner that he can't go on any more, when enter Pierre with bell )

sp.speaker: Pierre.
sp.p: Hall in to supper. "'Tis a knell as summons yer to Evins or to"—well, Divine Williams.

div.stage: (Music—Feversham leads band—all dance off in mock procession—Grigson prodding Pierre with his trident—exeunt Omnes )
Enter Marks in domino, L.

sp.speaker: Marks.
sp.p: Here's the rose bower. (goes to rose bower, and opens door, shewing another entrance on the other side) Ha! another door leading into a dark walk. That's probably the one my Countess will choose ; so I'll set the trap that way. (closes door, and disappears within—Music faintly heard in distance)

div.stage: Enter Edith dressed as a girl, R. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Edith.
sp.p: He is to fly with her to-night. I still dare to hope 'tis in my power to prevent him. Oh! if he loves me! He is here, and I have scarcely courage to meet him! (leans against balustrade at back, L.)

div.stage: Enter Lester, R., hastily.

sp.speaker: Lester.
sp.p: To what mad act has my confounded impetuosity led me! A quarter to twelve—and at twelve—I almost wish that—Oh, Edith! Edith! your love might have saved me from this! What makes me think of her now? Ah! of course; the strange likeness in that boy Antoine! Ah, well! it's my own act, and I might as well begin regretting my whole life at once.

sp.speaker: Edith.
